content.text: Recursive character splitting divides text every N tokens regardless of meaning. A 500-token boundary frequently lands mid-explanation, separating a concept from its definition or a code example from the paragraph that contextualises it. The resulting chunks embed a mixture of ideas, making them harder to match precisely and harder to use as grounded context. Semantic chunking fixes this by treating text as a sequence of ideas rather than a sequence of tokens: it splits at the boundaries where the topic changes, keeping related sentences together in the same chunk.

attrs.alt: Semantic chunking diagram: fixed-size splits cut mid-topic; semantic splits align with cosine similarity drops between adjacent sentences
attrs.title: Semantic Chunking

content.text: The algorithm embeds each sentence individually, then computes the cosine similarity between each adjacent pair. Where similarity drops sharply — indicating a topic shift — a chunk boundary is inserted. Sentences within the same topic cluster into one chunk; the chunk boundary aligns with the natural transition point. The result is variable-length chunks that are semantically coherent: each chunk discusses one coherent concept regardless of how many tokens that takes.

# pip install langchain-experimental
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

# percentile: split where similarity drops below the Nth percentile of all similarities
chunker = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",  # "percentile" | "standard_deviation" | "interquartile"
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=95,           # split at drops below the 95th percentile
)

with open("technical_doc.txt") as f:
    text = f.read()

chunks = chunker.create_documents([text])
print(f"Fixed splitter would give ~{len(text)//500} chunks")
print(f"Semantic chunker gives {len(chunks)} chunks")
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks[:3]):
    print(f"\nChunk {i+1} ({len(chunk.page_content)} chars):")
    print(chunk.page_content[:120], "...")

SemanticChunker supports three strategies for detecting topic-shift breakpoints. The percentile strategy splits at drops below the Nth percentile of all similarity scores in the document — robust and easy to tune. The standard_deviation strategy splits wherever similarity drops more than N standard deviations below the mean — more sensitive to local variation. The interquartile strategy uses the IQR fence (Q1 - 1.5×IQR) — suitable for corpora with heavy-tailed similarity distributions.

from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

# Percentile: stable, good default — splits at the bottom N% of similarities
chunker_pct = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=90,  # lower = more splits; higher = fewer, larger chunks
)

# Standard deviation: good for uniformly dense technical text
chunker_std = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="standard_deviation",
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=1.5,  # split if drop > 1.5 σ below mean
)

# Interquartile: good for mixed-density corpora (some dense, some sparse sections)
chunker_iqr = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="interquartile",
)

# Compare chunk counts
for name, c in [("pct", chunker_pct), ("std", chunker_std), ("iqr", chunker_iqr)]:
    result = c.create_documents([text])
    sizes = [len(d.page_content) for d in result]
    print(f"{name}: {len(result)} chunks, avg {sum(sizes)//len(sizes)} chars")
Building a Retriever from Semantic Chunks

Once documents are semantically chunked, the rest of the RAG pipeline is unchanged. Embed each chunk, store it in a vector store, and retrieve with standard similarity search. Because each chunk is semantically coherent, embeddings are more focused and retrieval precision improves — especially for multi-sentence queries that would previously have matched fragments of several different fixed-size chunks.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()
chunker = SemanticChunker(embeddings, breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
                          breakpoint_threshold_amount=90)

# Load and split
raw_docs: list[Document] = load_your_documents()
semantic_chunks: list[Document] = []
for doc in raw_docs:
    chunks = chunker.create_documents(
        [doc.page_content],
        metadatas=[doc.metadata] * 1,  # broadcast metadata to all chunks
    )
    semantic_chunks.extend(chunks)

# Index
vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(semantic_chunks, embeddings)
retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 5})

# Query — results are topic-coherent chunks
results = retriever.invoke("how does positional encoding work in transformers")
for r in results:
    print(len(r.page_content), r.page_content[:100])

Semantic chunking embeds every sentence during the splitting step, which is significantly more expensive than recursive character splitting. For a 100-page document (~50 000 tokens), splitting produces ~700–1000 sentences, each requiring an embedding API call. In practice, sentence embeddings are batched and the embedding cost is incurred only once at index time — not at query time. Use a cheaper embedding model (text-embedding-3-small) for the chunking step if the splitter model differs from the retrieval model; consistency between chunking and retrieval embeddings is more important than model tier.

Semantic chunking can produce very large chunks when a document has a long single-topic section, or very small chunks when many one-sentence paragraphs follow each other. Add min_chunk_size and max_chunk_size guardrails: if a proposed chunk falls below the minimum, merge it with its neighbour; if it exceeds the maximum, apply a secondary recursive split. This prevents degenerate chunk sizes while still respecting semantic boundaries where possible.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

def semantic_chunk_with_guardrails(
    text: str,
    chunker: SemanticChunker,
    min_chars: int = 200,
    max_chars: int = 2000,
) -> list[str]:
    """Semantic chunking with min/max size guardrails."""
    raw_chunks = [d.page_content for d in chunker.create_documents([text])]

    # Merge chunks that are too small (below min_chars)
    merged = []
    buffer = ""
    for chunk in raw_chunks:
        buffer = (buffer + " " + chunk).strip() if buffer else chunk
        if len(buffer) >= min_chars:
            merged.append(buffer)
            buffer = ""
    if buffer:
        merged.append(buffer)

    # Split chunks that are too large (above max_chars)
    fallback_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
        chunk_size=max_chars, chunk_overlap=50
    )
    final = []
    for chunk in merged:
        if len(chunk) > max_chars:
            final.extend(fallback_splitter.split_text(chunk))
        else:
            final.append(chunk)

    return final

chunks = semantic_chunk_with_guardrails(text, chunker, min_chars=300, max_chars=1500)
print(f"{len(chunks)} chunks, sizes: {[len(c) for c in chunks[:5]]}")

If you need more control than SemanticChunker provides — custom sentence splitters, local embedding models, or different distance metrics — you can implement the similarity-based chunking algorithm directly. The algorithm is straightforward: tokenise into sentences, embed in batch, compute adjacent cosine similarities, find the breakpoint indices, and split the sentence list at those indices.

import numpy as np
import nltk
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

nltk.download("punkt", quiet=True)

def cosine_similarity(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-8))

def semantic_chunk(
    text: str,
    model_name: str = "all-MiniLM-L6-v2",
    percentile: float = 90.0,
) -> list[str]:
    """Split text into semantic chunks using local embedding model."""
    model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
    sentences = nltk.sent_tokenize(text)
    if len(sentences) < 2:
        return sentences

    # Batch embed all sentences
    embeddings = model.encode(sentences, batch_size=64, show_progress_bar=False)

    # Compute adjacent cosine similarities
    similarities = [
        cosine_similarity(embeddings[i], embeddings[i + 1])
        for i in range(len(embeddings) - 1)
    ]

    # Find breakpoints: positions where similarity drops below the threshold
    threshold = np.percentile(similarities, 100 - percentile)
    breakpoints = [i + 1 for i, s in enumerate(similarities) if s < threshold]

    # Split sentence list at breakpoints
    chunks, prev = [], 0
    for bp in breakpoints:
        chunks.append(" ".join(sentences[prev:bp]))
        prev = bp
    chunks.append(" ".join(sentences[prev:]))
    return [c for c in chunks if c.strip()]

chunks = semantic_chunk(long_text, percentile=90)
print(f"{len(chunks)} chunks")

Semantic chunking delivers the most benefit when your corpus is dense and topically heterogeneous — research papers, textbooks, legal documents, or technical manuals where a single section spans many topics and a fixed-size chunk will almost always straddle a topic boundary. It is less effective on structured documents where each section is already a natural chunk (Markdown files with headings, HTML pages, structured JSON), since the structure already provides clean boundaries. In those cases, use a structure-aware splitter (MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter, HTMLHeaderTextSplitter) instead.

from langchain_text_splitters import MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter

# Structured docs: use header-based splitting instead of semantic chunking
md_splitter = MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter(
    headers_to_split_on=[
        ("#", "h1"),
        ("##", "h2"),
        ("###", "h3"),
    ]
)
# Each chunk aligns with a heading section — already semantically coherent

# Unstructured prose: use semantic chunking
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

semantic_splitter = SemanticChunker(
    OpenAIEmbeddings(),
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=90,
)

# Decision: check if your doc has reliable section structure
def choose_splitter(doc_text: str, has_headers: bool):
    if has_headers:
        return md_splitter
    return semantic_splitter

Two proxy metrics help evaluate chunk quality before running expensive end-to-end RAG evaluation. Intra-chunk similarity measures the average cosine similarity between all sentence pairs within a chunk — higher means the chunk is more coherent. Inter-chunk similarity measures the similarity between the last sentence of one chunk and the first sentence of the next — lower means the boundary was placed correctly. Good semantic chunks have high intra-similarity and low inter-similarity at boundaries.

import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import nltk

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

def chunk_quality_metrics(chunks: list[str]) -> dict:
    """Compute intra-chunk cohesion and inter-chunk separation."""
    intra_scores = []
    for chunk in chunks:
        sents = nltk.sent_tokenize(chunk)
        if len(sents) < 2:
            continue
        embs = model.encode(sents)
        # Average pairwise similarity within chunk
        sims = [
            float(np.dot(embs[i], embs[j]) /
                  (np.linalg.norm(embs[i]) * np.linalg.norm(embs[j]) + 1e-8))
            for i in range(len(embs))
            for j in range(i + 1, len(embs))
        ]
        intra_scores.append(np.mean(sims))

    # Boundary similarity: last sentence of chunk k vs first of chunk k+1
    boundary_scores = []
    for k in range(len(chunks) - 1):
        last  = nltk.sent_tokenize(chunks[k])[-1]
        first = nltk.sent_tokenize(chunks[k + 1])[0]
        e1, e2 = model.encode([last, first])
        sim = float(np.dot(e1, e2) / (np.linalg.norm(e1) * np.linalg.norm(e2) + 1e-8))
        boundary_scores.append(sim)

    return {
        "intra_cohesion": round(np.mean(intra_scores), 4),
        "boundary_separation": round(np.mean(boundary_scores), 4),
        "n_chunks": len(chunks),
    }

metrics = chunk_quality_metrics(chunks)
print(metrics)
# {'intra_cohesion': 0.7823, 'boundary_separation': 0.4102, 'n_chunks': 14}
Semantic chunking splits at cosine similarity drops between adjacent sentences, aligning boundaries with topic changes rather than token counts.
Index-time cost is higher than fixed splitting — sentence embeddings are computed during chunking.
Apply min/max guardrails to prevent degenerate chunk sizes at the extremes.
Best suited for dense, heterogeneous documents where topic shifts do not align with paragraph breaks.
